stories.paragraph: In the spring of 1915 the Tatosian family fled the city of Van during the Armenian Genocide. Father Hagop, a photographer, carried a leather case holding his glass plates; mother Araxi carried their youngest son Vahan; two older children walked at their side. Smoke rose behind them over the stone houses of the old Armenian quarter.

For many months the family moved south with a long caravan of survivors through the deserts of Syria and Sudan. They rode on an exhausted camel, Hagop walking beside it with a wooden stick. The line of refugees stretched across a bare horizon of sand and dry hills.

In 1922 the family reached Addis Ababa, where a small Armenian community met them outside the city. A priest in a black robe embraced Araxi; Ethiopian onlookers in white shammas watched with quiet sympathy. Behind them the highland hills rose green under a bright sky.

On a Sunday the family climbed the steps of Saint George's Cathedral on a hill above the city. Hagop and his sons stood on one side in dark suits; Araxi and her daughters stood on the other in white shammas draped over their heads. Ethiopian and Armenian worshippers moved together under painted icons.

Hagop opened a photography studio on the Piazza of Addis Ababa, its painted sign reading TATOSIAN PHOTOGRAPHE. He stood behind a large wooden bellows camera, a black cloth draped over his head; an Ethiopian army officer in a plumed helmet posed stiffly on a carved chair before a painted backdrop.

The Tatosian children played in the dirt lane outside the studio with Ethiopian neighbors, chasing a cloth ball. Vahan, the youngest, counted aloud in Amharic as he ran; his sister called him back in Armenian. An elderly neighbor woman watched from a doorway, grinding coffee in a wooden mortar.

As a young man, Vahan became apprentice to his father in the imperial court's photography service. He stood beside Hagop in a hall of the Jubilee Palace, adjusting a tripod as Emperor Haile Selassie, in a white cape and dark uniform, sat for an official portrait. Palace guards in white tunics stood along the walls.

In a courtyard in the Kazanchis neighborhood, Vahan attended a buna coffee ceremony at the home of an Ethiopian Orthodox priest. The priest's daughter, Almaz Wolde, knelt on a straw mat before a small charcoal brazier, roasting green coffee beans. She looked up to offer Vahan the first cup, incense drifting between them.

In 1934 Vahan and Almaz married in a ceremony that joined two Orthodox traditions. An Armenian priest and an Ethiopian Orthodox priest stood side by side before the couple, each placing a crown on their heads. Guests in white shammas and dark suits filled the small church; choirs of two traditions sang together.

In 1936, during the Italian occupation, the family hid in a farmhouse in the highlands outside Addis Ababa. Hagop, very old now, sat by a small hearth holding a wrapped bundle of glass plates; Vahan stood at a shuttered window watching a distant Italian column move along a road. Almaz held their first child on her lap.

After liberation in 1941 the family reopened the Piazza studio with a new painted sign. Vahan, now the master photographer, arranged a portrait of Emperor Haile Selassie surrounded by his generals and ministers; Almaz stood behind the counter organizing prints. Sunlight fell through a high window onto the polished wooden floor.

In 2015 the great-grandchildren of the Tatosian-Wolde family gathered in the courtyard of the old studio from four countries: Ethiopia, Armenia, France, and the United States. They held framed photographs their great-grandfather had taken a century before. An old coffee brazier burned at the center of the circle, incense rising into the jacaranda branches above.
